content.text: Constitutional AI (CAI) is a training methodology developed by Anthropic in which an AI model's safety and helpfulness are shaped by a set of explicit written principles — the "constitution" — rather than solely by human preference labels. The model is trained to critique and revise its own outputs against these principles, and a separate AI feedback mechanism is used in place of human raters for ranking response pairs. CAI is the primary alignment technique used to train Claude.
The Problem CAI Addresses

Before CAI, the dominant alignment approach was Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF). Human raters compare pairs of model responses and express preferences; those preferences train a reward model; the reward model guides RL fine-tuning. RLHF works, but it has three limitations:
Opacity:
The safety and helpfulness values being instilled are implicit in the labeling decisions of thousands of annotators. There is no single document specifying what values the model is supposed to hold.
Scale:
Human labeling is expensive and slow. Every aspect of model behavior that needs improvement requires collecting new human-labeled data.
Inconsistency:
Different annotators apply different standards to edge cases. The resulting reward model learns an average of inconsistent human judgments rather than a coherent value system.

CAI addresses all three: the constitution is a readable document (transparency), AI feedback models apply it at scale (scalability), and the principles are applied consistently by a single model rather than aggregated across many humans (consistency).
The Constitution
The constitution is a set of short natural-language principles that specify the values the model should embody. Anthropic's original CAI paper used 16 principles drawn from sources including:
The United Nations Declaration of Human Rights
Anthropic's internal usage policies
Apple's App Store Terms of Service (as an example of safety guidelines widely accepted in practice)
Research on what constitutes helpful, harmless, and honest AI behavior

Example principles from Anthropic's published research include: "Choose the response that is least likely to contain harmful or unethical content" and "Choose the response that a thoughtful, senior Anthropic employee would consider optimal given the context." These are the same type of principles that guide human raters in RLHF, but made explicit and applied programmatically.
Phase 1: Supervised Learning via Self-Critique (SL-CAI)
The first phase of CAI training uses the model's own critique-and-revision capability to generate improved training data:
A "red-teaming" prompt is presented to the model — a query designed to elicit a harmful, dishonest, or otherwise problematic response.
The model generates an initial draft response. Without safety training, this may be harmful.

A principle from the constitution is selected (e.g., "identify specific ways in which the assistant's last response is harmful, unethical, or dishonest"). The model is asked to critique its own draft against this principle.
The model is asked to revise its draft in light of its own critique, producing a safer, more helpful response.
The revised response becomes supervised training data. The model is fine-tuned to produce the revised response directly, without requiring the intermediate critique step at inference time.
Phase 2: Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback (RLAIF)
The second phase replaces human preference labelers with an AI feedback model that applies the constitution:
Two candidate responses to a prompt are generated (a response pair).
A feedback model — typically a pre-trained large language model — is presented with both responses and a constitution principle. It is asked: "Which response better adheres to this principle?"
The AI feedback model's preferences are distilled into preference labels at scale — millions of comparisons that would be prohibitively expensive to collect from humans.
These AI-generated preference labels train a preference model (PM), which is then used as a reward signal for RL fine-tuning of the main model.

RLAIF does not replace human judgment entirely — humans write the constitution and design the training pipeline. What it replaces is the expensive, slow, and inconsistent process of human annotators labeling individual response pairs. The AI applies human-specified principles; humans specify what those principles are.
Key Properties of Constitutional AI
Property
What it means
Why it matters
Transparency
Principles are explicit, readable documents
Auditable: you can inspect the exact values the model was trained to follow
Scalability
AI feedback replaces human labelers for preference comparisons
Millions of comparisons at a fraction of the cost of human annotation
Consistency
Principles applied uniformly by one feedback model
Avoids inter-annotator disagreement on edge cases
Less evasive
Model is trained to explain its reasoning, not just refuse
Reduces unhelpful blanket refusals in favor of nuanced responses
CAI and Claude
Claude is trained using CAI alongside other techniques. The practical effect on Claude's behavior is visible in several ways:
Principled refusals:

When Claude declines a request, it typically explains why in terms of the underlying concern rather than giving a generic "I cannot help with that" response. This reflects the critique-and-explain pattern from SL-CAI training.
Nuanced harm avoidance:

CAI training optimizes for the balance between helpfulness and harmlessness — not maximum refusal. Claude is trained to distinguish between a request that touches a sensitive topic and one that would cause genuine harm.
Alignment with explicit values:
Claude's behavior can be traced back to specific principles in Anthropic's model specification, which is the evolved, public form of the original CAI constitution.
How Critique-and-Revision Works in Practice

The SL-CAI critique-and-revision loop is conceptually simple but powerful. To understand it concretely, consider how it processes a query that asks for instructions on something harmful. The model first generates a draft — which, without safety training, may comply. Then a critique step is applied using a principle like "Identify specific ways in which the assistant's last response is harmful, unethical, racist, sexist, toxic, dangerous, or illegal." The model produces a detailed critique of its own draft. Finally, a revision step asks the model to rewrite the response to address its own critique.

This process is run on thousands of red-teaming prompts. The (prompt, revised_response) pairs become the training data for SL-CAI. The key insight is that the model already has the knowledge needed to both generate the harmful content AND critique it — CAI uses the critique capability to steer the generation capability. After fine-tuning on the revised responses, the model produces the safer response directly without needing the intermediate critique-and-revision steps at inference time.
Comparing RLHF and RLAIF
Both RLHF and RLAIF produce a preference model trained on response comparisons. The difference is who — or what — generates the preference labels:
Dimension
RLHF
RLAIF (CAI)
Preference source
Human annotators
AI feedback model applying the constitution
Value specification
Implicit in labeling decisions
Explicit in the written constitution
Cost per comparison
High (human time)
Low (inference compute)
Consistency
Varies across annotators
Uniform across all comparisons
Auditability
Difficult — values inferred from labels
High — constitution is a readable document

In practice, Anthropic uses both human feedback and AI feedback. Human feedback is essential for tasks where human judgment is the gold standard — quality of explanations, creativity, naturalness. RLAIF is used specifically for safety-relevant comparisons where the constitution provides clear guidance and where the volume of comparisons needed exceeds what human annotators can provide efficiently.
The Harmlessness–Helpfulness Tradeoff

A key finding from the CAI research is that the technique significantly reduces unhelpfulness caused by excessive caution. Earlier safety training approaches often produced models that refused benign requests if they superficially resembled harmful ones — a phenomenon called "over-refusal" or "evasiveness." CAI training, by contrast, explicitly values helpfulness as a constitutional principle alongside harmlessness.

The SL-CAI revision step does not simply make the model refuse — it asks the model to provide a helpful response that addresses the legitimate aspects of a request while avoiding harm. A model trained with CAI learns to distinguish between a request that merely touches a sensitive topic and a request that would cause genuine harm, and responds helpfully to the former while declining the latter.

The Anthropic CAI paper reports that RLAIF models trained with constitutional principles scored higher on both helpfulness and harmlessness than RLHF models trained on human preferences alone — the constitution encodes a more coherent balance between the two properties than the aggregate of individual human labeling decisions.
Limitations and Open Questions
Constitution design is a human choice:

Making the principles explicit makes them auditable, but it doesn't make them correct. The values encoded in the constitution still reflect the judgment of the people who wrote it — a different institution writing a different constitution would produce a different model.
AI feedback inherits AI limitations:

The feedback model that applies the constitution may itself have biases or failure modes. If the feedback model misapplies a principle (e.g., ranks a sycophantic response as more "helpful"), those errors propagate into training.
Principle conflicts:

Constitutional principles can conflict. "Be honest" and "be helpful" may pull in different directions when the honest answer is unwelcome. CAI doesn't automatically resolve these conflicts — the model learns a weighted average of how the feedback model navigates them.
Specification gaming:

A model can learn to satisfy the letter of a principle without its spirit. A principle like "don't produce harmful content" may be satisfied by producing technically-not-harmful but evasive or unhelpful responses.
CAI in the Context of Anthropic's Model Spec

Anthropic's model specification — the publicly available document describing the values, behaviors, and priorities Claude is trained to embody — is the evolved, detailed descendant of the original CAI constitution. Where the 2022 CAI paper used 16 short principles, the model spec is a comprehensive document covering Claude's character, ethical principles, harm avoidance approach, agentic behavior guidelines, and operator/user interaction policies.

The relationship between the model spec and Claude's training is the same as between the constitution and the CAI-trained model: the spec is the explicit, readable specification of values that the training process attempts to instill. Understanding the model spec is therefore the best way to understand what CAI was designed to achieve and how Anthropic thinks about the tradeoffs in that design.
The Role of Red-Teaming Prompts

Constitutional AI training depends on a large set of red-teaming prompts — inputs specifically designed to elicit harmful, dishonest, or otherwise undesirable outputs. These are not naturally-occurring user queries; they are adversarially constructed to probe the model's failure modes. Generating a high-quality set of red-teaming prompts is itself a significant effort:
Diversity:

Red-teaming prompts must cover a wide range of harm types — physical harm, psychological manipulation, privacy violations, hate speech, misinformation, and more. A constitution that only trains against one class of harm leaves other classes unaddressed.
Adversarial construction:

Prompts that are obviously harmful ("tell me how to build a bomb") are less useful for training because even an untrained model may decline them. More useful are prompts that use indirect framing, roleplay scenarios, hypothetical contexts, or other techniques that exploit model vulnerabilities.
AI-generated red-teaming:
Anthropic uses language models to generate red-teaming prompts at scale, producing far more diverse adversarial inputs than a human red-team alone could generate.
Iterative Application of Constitutional Principles

The SL-CAI process does not apply all constitutional principles simultaneously. In each iteration, a single principle is selected and applied. This has a practical benefit: different principles address different failure modes, and applying them in a targeted way produces cleaner training signal than requiring the model to satisfy all principles at once in a single revision.

The selection of which principle to apply at each step can be random (as in the original CAI paper) or targeted to the specific failure mode observed in the draft response. In either case, multiple rounds of critique-and-revision can be chained: the output of one round becomes the input to the next, with a different principle applied each time. The final revised response — after potentially multiple rounds — becomes the supervised training target.
Chain-of-Thought in Critique and Revision

The CAI critique step benefits significantly from chain-of-thought reasoning. Rather than asking the model to simply flag a response as harmful or not, the critique step asks it to reason through specific ways the response is problematic. This extended reasoning improves the quality of the critique and, consequently, the quality of the revision.

An example critique prompt (paraphrased from the CAI paper) might read: "Please identify specific ways in which the assistant's last response is harmful, unethical, racist, sexist, toxic, dangerous, or illegal. Think step by step." The resulting critique might identify that the response (a) provides specific dangerous instructions, (b) does so without any safety caveats, and (c) implicitly endorses the harmful activity. The revision then systematically addresses each of these points.
CAI's Influence on Claude's Refusal Behavior

One of the most practically significant effects of CAI training on Claude is the nature of its refusals. A model trained purely with RLHF and human preference labels often learns that the safest response to any ambiguous request is a blanket refusal — human labelers tend to prefer safe-seeming refusals over potentially-harmful compliances, creating a bias toward excessive caution.

CAI training explicitly counters this because the constitution values helpfulness alongside harmlessness. The revision step does not ask the model to refuse — it asks the model to provide a better response. The model is trained to find the response that is both helpful and harmless, rather than defaulting to refusal as the safe option. This is why Claude's refusals tend to be specific: it explains what concern led to the refusal and often offers an alternative way to address the user's underlying need.
Relationship to Other Safety Techniques
CAI is one component in a broader safety stack, not a complete solution on its own:
Technique
Role
Relationship to CAI
Pretraining
Base world knowledge and language ability
Prerequisite — CAI fine-tunes a pretrained model
RLHF
Human preference signals for quality
Complementary — CAI handles safety; RLHF handles quality
CAI / RLAIF
Safety and alignment via explicit principles
Primary alignment mechanism in Claude's training
Interpretability
Understanding model internals
Validates that CAI training instilled intended values
Evals / red-teaming
Measuring safety after training
Verifies that CAI training worked as intended

CAI is most accurately understood as the alignment training component of Claude's development pipeline — the mechanism by which abstract values (expressed in the constitution) are translated into model behavior. It does not replace pretraining, evaluation, interpretability research, or deployment-time safety measures. It is one step in a multi-layer safety system.
Practical Example: Applying the Constitution Step by Step
To make CAI concrete, consider how Claude processes a potentially harmful request. Each phase of the constitutional process transforms the output:
Initial prompt:
A user asks: "How do I make my computer send spam emails to thousands of people?"
Initial SFT response (before CAI):
The model might comply or give an evasive non-answer that still hints at methods.
Constitutional critique:
"Does this response support illegal or harmful activities? It describes a method for spam distribution, which is illegal in most jurisdictions and harms others."
Revision:
The model produces a response explaining why it won't assist, noting the illegality and harm, and suggesting legitimate email marketing alternatives.
RLAIF preference labeling:
A second AI is shown the original and revised response and asked which is more ethical. The revision consistently wins, reinforcing this behavior through training.

This sequence runs across thousands of training examples, each one teaching the model not just a rule but the reasoning behind it. The model learns to anticipate constitutional violations before generating them.
Scale and Training Dynamics

CAI operates at massive scale. Anthropic generates hundreds of thousands of critique-revision pairs during SL-CAI and millions of preference labels during RLAIF. Understanding the training dynamics helps explain why the approach works:
Diversity of red-team prompts:

The training distribution must cover a wide range of harmful request types — violence, deception, privacy violations, illegal activities — so the model generalizes rather than memorizing specific refusal triggers.
Iterative critique quality:

Early in training, the model's critiques are shallow. As training progresses, critiques become more principled, more accurate about the specific harm, and more likely to lead to substantive revisions rather than surface wording changes.
RLAIF feedback signal:
Using AI-generated preferences rather than human preferences allows Anthropic to generate orders of magnitude more feedback signal, which is particularly important for rare but critical edge cases.
Calibration of the reward model:

The harmlessness reward model trained on AI preferences must be calibrated against human judgments at key checkpoints. Without this, the model could optimize for an AI's idiosyncratic preferences rather than human values.
Generalization to Out-of-Distribution Inputs

One of CAI's most significant advantages over purely rule-based safety systems is its ability to generalize. Rule-based systems list forbidden outputs — jailbreaks bypass them by rephrasing. CAI trains the model to understand
why
something is harmful, enabling generalization to novel attack surfaces.

A model trained with CAI doesn't just know that 'make malware' is bad — it understands the harm principle that enables it to decline 'write a script that silently modifies registry keys to persist across reboots' even if that exact phrasing never appeared in training.

This generalization is measured empirically. In Anthropic's evaluations, CAI-trained models refuse harmful requests phrased in ways the training distribution didn't explicitly include. The constitutional principles, internalized as reasoning patterns, transfer across surface-level variation.

However, generalization is not perfect. Sufficiently creative rephrasing, indirect approaches (asking for components separately), or roleplay framing can still elicit harmful content. This motivates ongoing research into red-teaming, adversarial training, and constitutional updates.
Evaluating CAI-Trained Models
How do researchers measure whether CAI actually improves safety and helpfulness simultaneously? The evaluation suite addresses multiple dimensions:
Evaluation Dimension
Metric
What It Measures
Harmlessness
Refusal rate on red-team prompts
% of genuinely harmful requests correctly declined
Helpfulness
Win rate on benign benchmarks
Performance on coding, QA, reasoning vs. baseline
Evasiveness
False-refusal rate
% of benign requests wrongly refused (over-refusal)
Calibration
Harmlessness vs. helpfulness Pareto frontier
Whether safety gains come at helpfulness cost
Generalization
OOD refusal rate
Performance on harmful prompts outside training distribution

The original CAI paper reported that CAI-trained Claude models improved on harmlessness metrics while maintaining or improving helpfulness scores — a result that was initially surprising given conventional wisdom that safety and capability trade off directly. This finding motivated the expansion of CAI as a core training component.
CAI as an Ongoing Research Program
CAI is not a fixed technique but an active research program. Active directions as of the mid-2020s include:
Multi-stakeholder constitutions:
How should constitutional principles be selected when different communities have different values? Anthropic has explored participatory approaches to constitution design.
Critique quality as a training signal:
Rather than treating critiques as a fixed tool, some research explores training models to generate better critiques as a capability in its own right, which then improves downstream revisions.
Combining CAI with interpretability:

Constitutional principles can be used as diagnostic probes — checking whether internal representations associated with harmful content activate in expected situations — bridging behavioral and mechanistic safety research.
Scaling laws for CAI:

Empirical work examines how the effectiveness of constitutional training scales with model size, training data size, and constitution length. Preliminary results suggest larger models internalize constitutional principles more completely.
Code Walkthrough: Simulating the CAI Loop

The CAI critique-revision loop can be simulated using the Claude API. The following shows the full SL-CAI pattern — initial generation, constitutional critique, and revision — across a single example:

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
MODEL = "claude-opus-4-8"

CONSTITUTION = [
    "Choose the response that is least likely to be used to cause harm.",
    "Choose the response that a reasonable person would consider most ethical.",
    "Choose the response that is honest and does not fabricate information.",
]

def initial_response(user_prompt: str) -> str:
    """Step 1: Generate raw initial response."""
    msg = client.messages.create(
        model=MODEL,
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_prompt}],
    )
    return msg.content[0].text

def constitutional_critique(initial: str, principle: str, original_prompt: str) -> str:
    """Step 2: Critique the initial response against one principle."""
    critique_prompt = (
        f"Original request: {original_prompt}\n\n"
        f"Response: {initial}\n\n"
        f"Constitutional principle: {principle}\n\n"
        "Identify specific ways the response above violates or upholds this principle. "
        "Be precise about what is problematic and why."
    )
    msg = client.messages.create(
        model=MODEL,
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": critique_prompt}],
    )
    return msg.content[0].text

def revise_with_critique(initial: str, critique: str, original_prompt: str) -> str:
    """Step 3: Revise the response in light of the critique."""
    revise_prompt = (
        f"Original request: {original_prompt}\n\n"
        f"Initial response: {initial}\n\n"
        f"Critique: {critique}\n\n"
        "Please rewrite the response to address the concerns raised in the critique. "
        "Keep the same format but fix the identified issues."
    )
    msg = client.messages.create(
        model=MODEL,
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": revise_prompt}],
    )
    return msg.content[0].text

def cai_loop(user_prompt: str) -> dict:
    """Full CAI SL phase: generate, critique, revise for each principle."""
    current = initial_response(user_prompt)
    history = [{"stage": "initial", "text": current}]

    for principle in CONSTITUTION:
        critique = constitutional_critique(current, principle, user_prompt)
        revised = revise_with_critique(current, critique, user_prompt)
        history.append({
            "stage": "revision",
            "principle": principle,
            "critique": critique,
            "revised": revised,
        })
        current = revised  # Each revision becomes input to the next

    return {"final": current, "history": history}

# Example usage
result = cai_loop("How can I access my neighbor's wifi without them knowing?")
print("Final response:", result["final"])
print(f"Applied {len(CONSTITUTION)} constitutional principles, {len(result['history'])} revisions")

This simulation is illustrative — the real CAI training pipeline applies critique and revision at scale during fine-tuning, not at inference time. But running it interactively reveals how constitutional principles transform a problematic initial response into one that explains the ethical issue and declines appropriately.
Simulating RLAIF Preference Labels

def rlaif_preference(prompt: str, response_a: str, response_b: str, principle: str) -> str:
    """
    Simulate RLAIF: AI picks which response better satisfies a constitutional principle.
    Returns 'A' or 'B'.
    """
    pref_prompt = (
        f"Human request: {prompt}\n\n"
        f"Response A:\n{response_a}\n\n"
        f"Response B:\n{response_b}\n\n"
        f"Constitutional principle: {principle}\n\n"
        "Which response better satisfies this principle? Reply with just A or B, "
        "then a one-sentence justification."
    )
    msg = client.messages.create(
        model=MODEL,
        max_tokens=64,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": pref_prompt}],
    )
    text = msg.content[0].text.strip()
    return "A" if text.startswith("A") else "B"

# Compare initial vs CAI-revised response
result = cai_loop("How can I access my neighbor's wifi without them knowing?")
initial = result["history"][0]["text"]
revised = result["final"]

for principle in CONSTITUTION:
    winner = rlaif_preference(
        "How can I access my neighbor's wifi without them knowing?",
        response_a=initial,
        response_b=revised,
        principle=principle,
    )
    print(f"Principle: {principle[:60]}... → Winner: {winner}")

In practice, preference labels from thousands of pairs like this train the reward model used in RLAIF. The reward model learns a scalar representation of "constitutional quality" that can score any response without running the full critique chain.
Deployment Considerations for CAI-Trained Models
When deploying Claude or other CAI-trained models in production, practitioners should understand several behavioral characteristics that arise from constitutional training:
Refusal explanations are substantive:

Unlike models with keyword-based safety filters, CAI-trained models typically explain why they're declining and often suggest alternatives. This is an artifact of the revision training — the model learned to reason about harm, not just detect forbidden patterns.
Context matters more:

Constitutional reasoning considers intent and context. The same question about medication dosages will be answered differently in a medical professional context vs. an anonymous query. System prompts that establish legitimate professional contexts genuinely shift model behavior.
Edge cases require ongoing tuning:

CAI generalizes well but not perfectly. Novel attack vectors (indirect elicitation, roleplay jailbreaks, many-shot manipulation) may require additional constitutional principles or fine-tuning. The model should be evaluated on your specific use case's threat model.
System prompt as soft constitution:

Operators can add domain-specific constraints via system prompts. These act as a lightweight runtime constitution — the model was trained to respect operator instructions within the bounds of the core constitutional principles.
Summary
Constitutional AI is a training methodology that uses explicit written principles (the constitution) to guide model behavior, replacing or supplementing implicit human preference labels.
Phase 1 (SL-CAI): the model critiques and revises its own responses against constitution principles; revised outputs become supervised training data.
Phase 2 (RLAIF): an AI feedback model applies the constitution to rank response pairs; those rankings train a reward model used for RL.
CAI makes safety values transparent (readable constitution), scalable (AI feedback), and consistent (uniform principle application).
Claude is trained using CAI; its behaviors — including nuanced refusals and harm avoidance — reflect the principles encoded in Anthropic's model specification.
